did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates a concatenated deep learning model for skin lesion classification. The method integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to extract non-statistical image features and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) to process statistical handcrafted features (color moments and GLCM). The model was trained and tested to classify skin lesions into seven classes (including melanoma and benign nevi), achieving an accuracy of 98.1%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new concatenated deep learning model (CNN and MLP) for skin lesion classification. It details the methodology, dataset usage, training parameters (20 epochs, batch size 16), and reports new empirical results (98.1% accuracy) compared to a baseline single CNN model. Although published in a conference proceedings (ISCON 2021), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Concatenated Model with Deep Learning Technique for Skin Lesion Detection
journal: 2021 5th International Conference on Information Systems and Computer Networks (ISCON)
brief_summary: This study proposes a concatenated deep learning model for skin lesion classification to improve diagnostic accuracy. The architecture integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for non-statistical feature extraction with a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) processing statistical handcrafted features, including color moments and GLCM. The model was evaluated using the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven classes of skin lesions. Performance was assessed using precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy, comparing the hybrid approach against a baseline single CNN model. The proposed concatenated model achieved a classification accuracy of 98.1%, significantly outperforming the single CNN model's 86.1% accuracy. The results demonstrate that combining deep learning with statistical features enhances the detection and classification of malignant and benign skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of skin lesions (Vascular skin lesions, Actinic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopy images into seven diagnostic categories using a concatenated CNN and MLP model. The model outputs estimated probabilities via a Softmax layer for class prediction, achieving 98.1% accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, collected by multiple institutions.

use.training: Used for training the concatenated deep learning model (CNN and MLP) and the baseline single CNN model.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (20 epochs).
use.testing: Used for testing the models to evaluate performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-score, accuracy).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

HAM10000.notes: The concatenated model showed higher precision, recall, and F1-scores for individual classes compared to the single CNN model. AUC for each class was also better in the concatenated model.

architecture_used.main_model: Concatenated Deep Learning Model (Hybrid CNN and MLP)

cnn_details.description: Used to mine non-statistical deep features from RGB color images. Contains convolutional layers.
cnn_details.layers: Multiple convolutional layers (specific count not reported)
cnn_details.normalization: Two batch normalization layers added after the last two convolution layers
cnn_details.dropout: Dropout with probabilities 0.1, 0.3, 0.2, and 0.4 applied after the first, second, third, and fourth convolution layers respectively

mlp_details.description: Used to process statistical handcrafted features (color moments and GLCM).
mlp_details.dropout: Dropout with probabilities 0.2, 0.3, 0.3, and 0.4 applied after the four fully connected layers respectively

feature_extraction.statistical_features: Color moments (Mean, Standard Deviation, Skewness, Kurtosis) for R, G, B channels

architecture_details.integration: Outputs of the CNN model and the fully connected (MLP) model are consolidated at the last layer.

classification_or_output_head.description: Transforms real values into estimated probability ranging from 0 to 1 for multiclass classification.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Concatenated Deep Learning Model (Hybrid CNN and MLP)
preprocessing_techniques: Extraction of Region of Interest (ROI) from unwanted portions of image data
Calculation of statistical handcrafted features: Color moments (Mean, Standard Deviation, Skewness, Kurtosis) for R, G, B channels
Calculation of texture features: Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) features (Energy, Homogeneity, Dissimilarity)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper mentions that a generative adversarial network (GAN) has been proposed in other works to increase data size on the HAM10000 dataset, but it does not report using GAN or any other specific data augmentation techniques (geometric, color, sampling, etc.) in its own proposed concatenated CNN-MLP model.

conclusions: The proposed concatenated deep learning model, which integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for non-statistical feature extraction and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) for statistical handcrafted features (color moments and GLCM), achieved a classification accuracy of 98.1% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The concatenated model significantly outperformed the baseline single CNN model, which achieved an accuracy of 86.1%.

The hybrid model demonstrated superior performance compared to the single CNN model in terms of precision, recall, F1-score, test loss reduction (from 0.477 to 0.052), and Area Under the Curve (AUC) for individual classes.
Both micro-average and macro-average performance metrics were higher for the proposed hybrid model than for the single CNN model.
The authors acknowledge that future work should incorporate additional clinical features such as burns, itching, age, sex, medical history, and locality to further improve classification efficiency.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is a multi-source dataset; the paper does not report performance on external, independent validation datasets, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other clinical populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of the performance difference between the proposed concatenated model (98.1% accuracy) and the baseline single CNN model (86.1% accuracy).

The authors acknowledge that the model currently lacks integration of crucial clinical metadata (age, sex, medical history, locality, symptoms like itching/burns), which are noted as necessary for future improvements in classification efficiency.

The paper does not employ data augmentation techniques within its own proposed model's training pipeline, despite acknowledging GAN-based augmentation in related literature; this may contribute to the high reported accuracy but raises concerns about overfitting given the limited training epochs (20) and batch size (16).

The specific architecture of the CNN component is not fully detailed (e.g., number of layers, kernel sizes, specific layer types beyond 'convolutional layers'), making exact replication of the deep feature extraction process difficult.

The dataset class distribution is not explicitly analyzed for imbalance; while the HAM10000 dataset is known to be imbalanced, the paper does not report if class-weighted loss functions or resampling techniques were used to mitigate this, potentially biasing the high accuracy metrics towards majority classes.
